title: Best Free VPN 2026: Which Ones Are Actually Usable
description: Most free VPNs are either useless or dangerous. These are the few genuinely free options worth using, and what their real limitations are.
content: Free VPNs are everywhere, and most are not worth your time. Many free VPN providers fund operations through data collection, injecting ads, or selling browsing history to third parties. A VPN that collects your data defeats the purpose of using a VPN. This guide covers the handful of genuinely usable free options and is clear about their limitations.

ProtonVPN free plan is the best free VPN in 2026, and it is not close. ProtonVPN's free plan offers unlimited data on three server locations (US, Netherlands, Japan). There is no logging, no ads, no data selling, and the company is audited by Cure53. The limitations are real: no P2P, no streaming server access, slower speeds than paid plans, and only one device at a time. But for basic privacy protection and occasional use, it is genuinely free and genuinely trustworthy.

Windscribe free plan gives you 10 GB per month with server access in 10 countries. Windscribe also allows you to use a custom firewall rule builder on the free plan, which is unusual. The 10 GB limit covers around 10 hours of standard web browsing but is used up quickly by video streaming. Windscribe earns revenue from paid upgrades, not data selling. The privacy policy is transparent.

TunnelBear free plan offers 2 GB per month, which is tight. It is useful for one-time usage (connecting from a hotel network, accessing a blocked site once) but not as a daily driver. TunnelBear is audited annually by Cure53 and publishes the results in full. Owned by McAfee, which raises some eyebrows, but the audit results have been consistently clean.

What to avoid: Hola VPN uses a peer-to-peer model where your internet connection is used by other Hola users. This means strangers are routing traffic through your device. Several security researchers have documented how Hola has been used to conduct DDoS attacks using the devices of free-tier users. Never install it. Similarly, VPN apps with millions of downloads and no clear business model are almost always generating revenue from your data.
